title: Separate dissenting opinion of Carmen Salcedo Beltrán: Decision on the merits: International Federation of Associations of the Elderly (FIAPA) v. France, Complaint No. 205/2022
text: European Committee of Social Rights Comité européen des Droits sociaux DECISION ON THE MERITS Adoption: 18 March 2025 Notification: 14 May 2025 Publication: 15 September 2025 International Federation of Associations of the Elderly (FIAPA) v. France Complaint No. 205/2022 SEPARATE DISSENTING OPINION OF CARMEN SALCEDO BELTRÁN 1. I cannot agree with the Committee’s majority decisions with regard to all of the complainant organisation’s allegations in which it finds that there is no violation of Article 23 of the revised European Social Charter (the Charter). 2. To back up my dissenting opinion fully, I will begin by looking at the two main premises for the decision. The first is that the Committee has considered that the allegations made by FIAPA should be examined under Article 23 read alone and that no separate issue arises under Article E read in conjunction with Article 23, as Article 23 itself is the specific expression of the right “not to be discriminated against” on the grounds of advanced age (FIAPA v. France, Complaint No. 145/2017, decision on the merits of 22 May 2019, §§48-49) (§60). Therefore – and I would like to insist on this point because it relates to the reasons for my overall disagreement – according to the Committee itself, the allegations presented entail the examination of a situation of discrimination, specifically targeting elderly persons. 3. The second premise is that according to the Committee’s established case law on disputes about discrimination in matters covered by the Charter (Mental Disability Advocacy Centre (MDAC) v. Bulgaria, Complaint No. 41/2007, decision on the merits of 3 June 2008, §52; Associação Sindical dos Profissionais da Polícia (ASPP/PSP) v. Portugal, Complaint No. 179/2019, decision on the merits of 4 July 2023, §68), “the burden of proof should not rest entirely on the complainant and in the case of prima facie evidence of discrimination, the burden of proof should shift to the State Party to prove that no discrimination occurred (European Roma Rights Centre (ERRC) v. Belgium, Complaint No. 195/2020, decision on the merits of 1 July 2024, §75)”. 4. My discord is based firstly on the fact that, when examining the instant case, the Committee failed to apply the fundamental legal principle of non-discrimination law with regard to shifting or reversing the burden of proof. Shifting the burden of proof requires the complainant organisation to present reasonable prior evidence demonstrating the truth of the alleged facts. FIAPA provided ample evidence of this sort, deriving from judicial decisions from national authorities. The complaint therefore was based on verifiable facts. Accordingly, this evidence made it possible to infer that there was discrimination against this group. It was therefore for the Government to prove that this discrimination did not occur. 5. This is clearly demonstrated by the total lack of any reference to this principle anywhere in the decision. Furthermore, on several occasions, the Committee specifically talks of the need for the complainant organisation to provide “evidence” of its claims. This is the case for instance when it states that its allegations “are not substantiated by sufficient evidence” (§137), that “no evidence has been provided” (§148) and “FIAPA has not provided evidence” (§168). 6. Furthermore, this omission is not only a legal anomaly where it comes to the examination of the complaint but also a clear sign of a contradiction in the Committee’s arguments. The Committee states on the one hand that “FIAPA has not provided evidence/case-law examples to substantiate their allegation” and on the other that “FIAPA did not provide any evidence [or] data to demonstrate that the legal framework in force is not effectively implemented in practice” (§157). In other words, it requires data, evidence or case-law examples. 7. Yet, when other claims by the complainant organisation are examined, in which the very evidence which the Committee asks for in the above example is provided, it comes to the inconsistent conclusion that this evidence is not adequate or simply attaches no significance to it. It considers for instance that FIAPA’s allegations “are not substantiated by sufficient evidence, whether in terms of data or examples of domestic court decisions demonstrating the inadequate organisation of the judicial system concerning assisted decision-making for older persons and the insufficiency and ineffective implementation of guarantees regarding their right to make decisions for themselves” (§137), that “these two court decisions are not sufficient for the Committee”, that “the above-mentioned judgment does not in itself substantiate FIAPA’s allegation” (§155), that “the single case example (Appeal Court of Dijon, 20 March 2019) provided by FIAPA does not enable the Committee to establish that the implementation of the domestic provisions” breaches rights in general (§147), and that “the absence of any other information submitted by FIAPA in this regard” poses a problem (§149). 8. Elderly people are a particularly vulnerable social group, for whom cases of discrimination are frequent and structural. Inherent problems with evidence in this area must be seen in the light of these people’s personal limits, persistent negative stereotypes and prejudice towards them which are deeply and lastingly entrenched in society, their social isolation and the indifference with which they are often treated, which can go so far as rendering them invisible. 9. However, this fact is totally overlooked in the Committee’s overall assessment of the complaint, in which it finds in each case either that there is no violation for want of evidence or, where evidence is provided, that this evidence is insufficient. In the latter case, a fundamental issue to be addressed is the standard of proof which the majority of the Committee considers to be required to find a breach of the obligations under the Charter. 10. Secondly, the complaint alleges that there is discrimination against elderly people with regard to the right of access to justice, encompassing all the related aspects, from the initiation of proceedings up to the final verdict. The Committee has found that there was no violation in respect of any of the claims submitted by the complainant organisation. 11. For several years, both the national and international supervisory bodies have been progressively incorporating now well-established approaches into their deliberations, such as gender or child perspectives. Added to this now is the elderly persons’ perspective, which the demographic situation in Europe has rendered indispensable given that about 21.6% of the population are 65 or over. This perspective has made it possible to guarantee that the rights granted to this group are effective and to remedy existing inequalities. The Charter protects rights “not merely theoretically, but also in fact” (International Commission of Jurists (ICJ) v. Portugal, Complaint No. 1/1998, decision on the merits of 9 September 1999, §32; European Federation of National Organisations Working with the Homeless (FEANTSA) v. Slovenia, Complaint No. 53/2008, decision on the merits of 8 September 2009, §28). 12. The incorporation of this approach into the examination of facts to be ruled on has resulted in the adoption of a “differentiated approach”, based on the acknowledgment that only such a distinction can secure the effective enjoyment of the rights guaranteed. This approach has been explicitly applied to the right of access to justice. For instance, in its judgment on Teachers of Chañaral and other Municipalities v. Chile, Series C No. 443, of 10 November 2021, §148, the Inter-American Court of Human Rights enshrined this approach as a general principle of international law applying to elderly persons (see also IACHR, National Association of Discharged and Retired Employees of the National Tax Administration Superintendence (ANCEJUB-SUNAT) v. Peru, 21 November 2019, §79). This gives rise to an obligation on states to give the elderly “preferential treatment” and to set an “enhanced standard of promptness” in all administrative and judicial proceedings (Teachers of Chañaral and other Municipalities v. Chile, cited above, §§ 147-152), this being the only means of affording them timely and effective access. 13. The European Court of Human Rights has also found that states must show “particular diligence” towards this group, placing emphasis on the issue at stake in view of the applicant’s age and the impact that ordinary judicial procedure may have, especially in terms of the length of the investigation (Jablonská v. Poland, No. 60225/00, judgment of 9 March 2004, §43). 14. The Committee states specifically in its decision that “in interpreting the provisions of the Charter, the Committee has to consider not only current conditions and relevant international instruments, but also emerging issues and situations” (§121). Under the established case law of the Committee, it interprets the rights and freedoms set out in the Charter in the light of current conditions (Marangopoulos Foundation for Human Rights (MFHR) v. Greece, Collective Complaint No. 30/2005, decision on the merits of 6 December 2006, §194), and of international instruments and the interpretations made of these treaties by their respective regulatory bodies (European Federation of National Organisations working with the Homeless (FEANTSA) v. France, Complaint No. 39/2006, decision on the merits of 5 December 2007, §64), bearing in mind that the Charter is a living instrument. This means that the Charter must be interpreted according to this context, namely international law and practice, of which it forms an integral part (Article 31§3 c) of the Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties, 1969) (ECHR, Golder v. the United Kingdom, no 4451/70, 21 February 1975, §§30 and 35). 15. This is only a theoretical reference (§121), as confirmed in the preceding paragraphs. It is regrettable that the Committee did not take the opportunity that this complaint afforded it to incorporate this elderly-person perspective and make Article 23 fully effective through harmonisation with the criteria adopted by its international counterparts. In my opinion, this group’s particular vulnerability was only considered from a theoretical viewpoint. Full and genuine protection would have involved setting priorities and implementing special measures in a context in which the complainant organisation demonstrated the existence of discrimination affecting various aspects such as investigative bodies, procedures or stakeholders, in other words all the components that make up a judicial procedure, bearing in mind their specific nature. 16. Until the Committee incorporates an “elderly perspective” and the legal principle of the reversal of the burden of proof into its case law on the rights of the elderly, there will continue to be a flagrant gap between the right enshrined in Article 23 and the actual circumstances. As this case illustrates, the level of protection afforded by Europe’s main social rights treaty is currently more limited than that provided by other international instruments. Moreover, this phenomenon is bound to become more pronounced. The United Nations predicts that in 2050, the number of people aged 65 over in the world will be double that of children under the age of five and practically the same as the number of children under the age of 12. World Population Prospects 2022, https://www.un.org/development/desa/pd/sites/www.un.org.development.desa.pd/files/wpp2022_summary_of_results.pdf According to European Union forecasts, the proportion of the population aged 65 or over will reach about 30% by 2050, compared to about 20% currently. The impact of demographic change in Europe, https://commission.europa.eu/strategy-and-policy/priorities-2019-2024/new-push-european-democracy/impact-demographic-change-europe_en 17. For the reasons outlined above, I voted for a finding of a violation of Article 23 of the Charter in respect of all the complaints.
complainant: International Federation of Associations of the Elderly (FIAPA)
